title: AI From Algorithms to AI Factories
subtitle: A Coursera-style deep dive into the software, hardware, and infrastructure behind modern AI
description: This course teaches the most important foundations in AI, spanning statistical learning, deep learning, foundation models, chips, distributed systems, and datacenter operations.
modules: Classical AI, uncertainty, and sequential decision-making
Foundation models and modern AI systems

lessons.title: What AI is: history, paradigms, and why the field looks the way it does now
lessons.summary: This lesson explains the central ideas behind what ai is: history, paradigms, and why the field looks the way it does now and connects the research lineage to modern AI practice.
objectives: Explain the key intuition behind what ai is: history, paradigms, and why the field looks the way it does now.
Connect the assigned papers to the historical arc of AI.
Use the concepts from this lesson to reason about current AI systems.

papers.title: Jordan and Mitchell, Trends and prospects

lessonPlan: Start by mapping the lesson topic, what ai is: history, paradigms, and why the field looks the way it does now, to the broader AI stack of algorithms, systems, and deployment.
Define the core terms carefully and contrast them with neighboring concepts so the student can build a precise mental model.
Walk through the key papers in chronological order, emphasizing what each one contributed and what limitation it addressed.
Translate the papers into practical engineering consequences: what changes in model design, system design, or evaluation once these ideas are understood.
End with a synthesis section that explains why the topic still matters for frontier AI today.

quiz.prompt: Which statement best captures the main goal of the lesson on what ai is: history, paradigms, and why the field looks the way it does now?
options: To memorize benchmarks without understanding system implications
To understand the topic conceptually and connect it to modern AI practice
To focus only on historical papers and ignore current relevance
To replace all empirical evaluation with intuition
quiz.explanation: Each lesson is designed to connect foundational research to current model, hardware, and systems decisions.

quiz.prompt: Why are the key papers arranged as a short curated list instead of a long reading dump?
options: Because the goal is to maximize recall with a manageable reading load
Because papers are optional and not relevant
Because long reading lists are always worse
Because implementation is more important than theory in every case
quiz.explanation: The course is optimized for signal rather than volume, so each reading should carry a large share of the lesson.

quiz.prompt: What should a student be able to do after completing the lesson plan?
options: Recite paper titles only
Explain definitions, compare ideas, and connect them to deployed AI systems
Avoid ambiguity by refusing to reason from incomplete evidence
Skip evaluation because the papers already prove the conclusions
quiz.explanation: The lesson plan is meant to produce usable understanding, not passive familiarity.

quiz.prompt: What is the role of the synthesis section at the end of each lesson?
options: It summarizes why the topic matters for frontier AI and product systems
It repeats the abstract of the first paper
It replaces the quiz
It removes the need to understand definitions
quiz.explanation: Synthesis helps students connect research lineage to current model, hardware, and product realities.

quiz.prompt: What is the best way to use the quiz in this course design?
options: As a memorization drill with no feedback
As a checkpoint for understanding with immediate explanations
As a ranking mechanism only
As a replacement for reading the papers
quiz.explanation: The quiz is meant to confirm understanding and reinforce concepts with feedback.

lessons.title: Robustness, safety, interpretability, and governance
lessons.summary: This lesson explains the central ideas behind robustness, safety, interpretability, and governance and connects the research lineage to modern AI practice.
objectives: Explain the key intuition behind robustness, safety, interpretability, and governance.
Connect the assigned papers to the historical arc of AI.
Use the concepts from this lesson to reason about current AI systems.

papers.title: Szegedy et al., Intriguing Properties of Neural Networks

lessonPlan: Start by mapping the lesson topic, robustness, safety, interpretability, and governance, to the broader AI stack of algorithms, systems, and deployment.
Define the core terms carefully and contrast them with neighboring concepts so the student can build a precise mental model.
Walk through the key papers in chronological order, emphasizing what each one contributed and what limitation it addressed.
Translate the papers into practical engineering consequences: what changes in model design, system design, or evaluation once these ideas are understood.
End with a synthesis section that explains why the topic still matters for frontier AI today.

quiz.prompt: Which statement best captures the main goal of the lesson on robustness, safety, interpretability, and governance?
options: To memorize benchmarks without understanding system implications
To understand the topic conceptually and connect it to modern AI practice
To focus only on historical papers and ignore current relevance
To replace all empirical evaluation with intuition
quiz.explanation: Each lesson is designed to connect foundational research to current model, hardware, and systems decisions.

quiz.prompt: Why are the key papers arranged as a short curated list instead of a long reading dump?
options: Because the goal is to maximize recall with a manageable reading load
Because papers are optional and not relevant
Because long reading lists are always worse
Because implementation is more important than theory in every case
quiz.explanation: The course is optimized for signal rather than volume, so each reading should carry a large share of the lesson.

quiz.prompt: What should a student be able to do after completing the lesson plan?
options: Recite paper titles only
Explain definitions, compare ideas, and connect them to deployed AI systems
Avoid ambiguity by refusing to reason from incomplete evidence
Skip evaluation because the papers already prove the conclusions
quiz.explanation: The lesson plan is meant to produce usable understanding, not passive familiarity.

quiz.prompt: What is the role of the synthesis section at the end of each lesson?
options: It summarizes why the topic matters for frontier AI and product systems
It repeats the abstract of the first paper
It replaces the quiz
It removes the need to understand definitions
quiz.explanation: Synthesis helps students connect research lineage to current model, hardware, and product realities.

quiz.prompt: What is the best way to use the quiz in this course design?
options: As a memorization drill with no feedback
As a checkpoint for understanding with immediate explanations
As a ranking mechanism only
As a replacement for reading the papers
quiz.explanation: The quiz is meant to confirm understanding and reinforce concepts with feedback.
